posts.text:
Other than that, Mrs. Lincoln, how was the play?


Ryan Petersen (@typesfast)

Guys, relax, the only risk in transiting the Strait of Hormuz is that Iran will shoot a missile at your ship. Otherwise it's open for transit. 

An all-time quote from our Secretary of War.

Video

posts.text: From a purely electoral standpoint (not that I don't have opinions about it from a non-electoral standpoint) Iran has to be one of the dumber moves in recent memory. Maybe the base case is that things get back to normal, but the risks for Trump are weighted to the downside.

posts.text:
You can check my Twitter feed for the prominent names I was fighting with about this over the weekend (a big VC and a TV host). But more importantly, people in the White House and the Defense Department seemed to be engaged in exactly the same sort of magical thinking.


Varad Mehta (@varadmehta)

"Fuck those people who said it would all be over in a few days."

So fuck a bunch of Twitter randos? That's a weird thing to say. Anyone know why Big Nate is crashing out so hard over this? Just seems

posts.text: "You can just do things" is basically the same heuristic as "Everyone has a plan until they get punched in the face" except for people who haven't been punched in the face yet.
posts.quote: "You can just do things" is basically the same heuristic as "Everyone has a plan until they get punched in the face" except for people who haven't been punched in the face yet.

posts.text: Obviously this stuff is hard to price. But the notion that it's *just* vibes is silly. It's *some* vibes mediated by objective facts on the ground amid the fog of war. Such as oil tankers (reportedly) being attacked. And the U.S. not really having a coherent gameplan.
posts.quote: Obviously this stuff is hard to price. But the notion that it's *just* vibes is silly. It's *some* vibes mediated by objective facts on the ground amid the fog of war. Such as oil tankers (reportedly) being attacked. And the U.S. not really having a coherent gameplan.

posts.text: While the dynamics between the GOP and Democrats aren't identical, one can identify some parallels to Gaza. Opinion among activists was a leading indicator for Democrats overall, though it took a year or two to really break through.
posts.quote: While the dynamics between the GOP and Democrats aren't identical, one can identify some parallels to Gaza. Opinion among activists was a leading indicator for Democrats overall, though it took a year or two to really break through.

posts.text: So far, polling on Iran doesn't show that much opposition among rank-and-file Republicans, even as people like Tucker Carlson and Joe Rogan denounce it. But as @emckowndawson points out, that may be missing the forest for the trees.

posts.text: Will Iran break MAGA? (by Eli McKown-Dawson). So far, there’s not much erosion in Trump’s polling among Republicans. But elite opinion can trickle down to the party base.

posts.text: War isn't what it once was. Past precedents are less useful than you'd think when predicting a war's political impact. But Iran still poses downside risks for Trump.

Other than that, Mrs. Lincoln, how was the play?


Ryan Petersen (@typesfast)

From a purely electoral standpoint (not that I don't have opinions about it from a non-electoral standpoint) Iran has to be one of the dumber moves in recent memory. Maybe the base case is that things get back to normal, but the risks for Trump are weighted to the downside.

You can check my Twitter feed for the prominent names I was fighting with about this over the weekend (a big VC and a TV host). But more importantly, people in the White House and the Defense Department seemed to be engaged in exactly the same sort of magical thinking.


Varad Mehta (@varadmehta)

"

It's a war so I'm gonna be very nice and not dunk on people who 100% deserve it but honestly fuck those people who said it would all be over in a few days.


Joe Weisenthal (@TheStalwart)

Oil tanker crossings through the Strait of Hormuz





— https://nitter.net/TheStalwart/status/2032103844759384
"You can just do things" is basically the same heuristic as "Everyone has a plan until they get punched in the face" except for people who haven't been punched in the face yet.

Obviously this stuff is hard to price. But the notion that it's *just* vibes is silly. It's *some* vibes mediated by objective facts on the ground amid the fog of war. Such as oil tankers (reportedly) being attacked. And the U.S. not really having a coherent gameplan.

While the dynamics between the GOP and Democrats aren't identical, one can identify some parallels to Gaza. Opinion among activists was a leading indicator for Democrats overall, though it took a year or two to really break through.

So far, polling on Iran doesn't show that much opposition among rank-and-file Republicans, even as people like Tucker Carlson and Joe Rogan denounce it. But as @emckowndawson points out, that may be missing the forest for the trees.
Will Iran break MAGA? (by Eli McKown-Dawson). So far, there’s not much erosion in Trump’s polling among Republicans. But elite opinion can trickle down to the party base.
War isn't what it once was. Past precedents are less useful than you'd think when predicting a war's political impact. But Iran still poses downside risks for Trump.

economy-trade.quote: Gas prices are set to go vertical. It's not exactly a bold prediction to suggest this will be bad for Trump's political standing.
